content: What are some business etiquette norms when doing business in Japan?

**A quick‑look guide to doing business in Japan**

| What | How to do it | Why it matters |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| **Meetings – First impressions** | Keep head bowed slightly, dress formal, arrive 5–10 min early | Respect & trust start with punctuality and a neat appearance |
| **Greeting & introductions** | Offer a firm handshake (if you do it), hand a business card (fukuro), cards on the left side of the face | The business card is “face” – it’s the first personal introduction |
| **Business cards** | When you receive a card, take it with both hands, look at it before storing it | Respect for the person who gave you the card, no one wants a card shoved in a pocket |
| **Choosing an appropriate greeting** | Say “はじめまして” (Hajimemashite, “Nice to meet you”), or “お世話になり” | Opens with a pleasant tone |
| **Talking style** | Use indirectness, avoid saying “no” directly, use terms like “maybe”, “perhaps” | “Negative” words can be perceived as confrontation |
| **Gift etiquette** | A small, modest gift after a meeting is appreciated. Avoid large expenses | Japanese appreciate modesty, showing humility |
| **Gift wrapping** | Present it wrapped in simple, elegant paper | Simple presentation shows thoughtfulness |
| **Meeting rules** | If you’re invited to a dinner, bring the right amount of money for drinks | It’s a courtesy for the host |
| **Business cards** | Exchange with both hands, keep a card in a good place, never reuse an old card | Respect for the recipient’s name & position |
| **Group decision-making** | Decision often takes time, “Yes” isn’t the answer – consensus is sought | Patience, group harmony |  

---

### 1.  First Impressions

| Do | Don't |
|----|-------|
| Bow at the entrance, 1–2 bows. | Skip the bow. |
| Arrive early, or on time. | Be late. |
| Dress conservatively, dark suit, tie for men. | Casual or flashy. |
| Use the title and last name, e.g., “Sato‑san”. | Ignore hierarchy or title. |

---

### 2.  Exchanging Business Cards (名刺交換 *meishaku*)

| Step | Etiquette |
|------|-----------|
| Slide the card on the fukuro (small card case). | Hold the card with both hands as you read it. |
| Write a note in your notebook (if you have one) to reference the contact. | Avoid writing on the card. |
| Keep the card in memory and refer to it afterwards | Avoid scrolling through your phone and printing new cards. |

---

### 3.  Communication Style

- **Indirectness** –  “はい” (yes) is not a firm “yes”, but a confirmation.  
- **Use honorifics** – 先生 *sensei* for your boss, *san* or *sama* for names.  
- **Avoid public criticism** –  “We can’t do that” →  “Perhaps we can approach it differently”.

---

### 4.  Gift Etiquette

| Occasion | Typical gift | Presentation | Tips |
|----------|--------------|-------------|------|
| After a meeting | A small gift (tea, regional specialty) | Neatly wrapped, no bright packaging | The gift is a token of thanks, not a bribe. |
| Holiday | Traditional goods or a small item from home | Use a *chōkan* (small gift basket) | Express gratitude, not obligation. |

---

### 5.  Meetings & Negotiations

| Common Japanese practice | Explanation |
|-------------------------|-------------|
| **Consensus** | Decision often comes after group discussion, so be patient. |
| **Formality** | A clear agenda, written material, and one‑to‑one meetings are valued. |
| **Silence** | A brief pause is okay; don’t fill silence with small talk. |

---

### 6.  Practical “Do’s & Don’ts”

| Do | Don’t |
|----|-------|
| Use *san* / *sama* with the name. | Use first names or nicknames. |
| Prepare agenda & bring hard copies. | Show the agenda only in English. |
| Make the first point first; keep agenda short. | Extemporaneous talk or vague proposals. |
| Let the senior person speak first. | Interrupt, especially when a senior speaks. |

---

### 7.  Quick Reference Cheat‑Sheet

| Practice | Quick Tip |
|---------|----------|
| Bows | 1‑2 shallow bows. |
| Meeting prep | Arrive 10 min early. |
| Gift acceptance | “Thank you” in Japanese, not “you are welcome”. |
| Dining | Use chopsticks properly; never stick them in rice. |

---

### Quick FAQ

| Question | Answer |
|----------|--------|
| **What is the proper name format?** | Last name first, then first name. |
| **Is a handshake common?** | Yes, but always bow first. |
| **Is “yes” the same as in the West?** | Not always - “yes” can mean “yes” or “yes, I’ll try.” |
| **Are meetings always formal?** | The first meetings are more formal; later will be relaxed. |
| **What about meeting etiquette?** | Bring a business card.  Bring your own envelope; no note‑to‑self. |

---

**Bottom line:**  
Japanese business culture values *respect*, *precision*, and *group harmony*. A quick bow, respectful language, a tidy card, and the right gift are your best “first‑nice” tickets to success.
Create a video script for training new employees of a car wash business in Japan. Highlight the above etiquette norms.

**Video Title:**  
**“Welcome to the Service Desk – Japanese Etiquette for New Car‑Wash Employees”**

---

### 1️⃣ Opening (00:00‑00:10)
- **Presenter (in a tidy uniform):**  
  “Welcome to the Car‑Wash Team! Today we’ll explore the simple habits that’ll set you apart and show respect to every customer we meet.”

---

### 2️⃣ Why Etiquette Matters? (00:10‑00:25)
- **Cut‑away footage:** Employees greeting customers in various settings, customers smiling.  
- **Voiceover:**  
  “In Japan, respect, timing, and modesty create trust. A courteous greeting can turn a simple wash into a memorable experience.”

---

### 3️⃣ Greeting & First Impressions (00:25‑01:00)
| Item | What to Do | Why It Matters |
|------|------------|----------------|
| **Bow** | Light bow, 1–2 seconds. | Shows humility and acknowledgment. |
| **Name & Title** | “~san” + last name. | Maintains hierarchy. |
| **Business Card** | Hand over with both hands, look at it before putting away. | Demonstrates attentiveness. |

*Visual cue:* “First‑class greeting visual” – a smiling employee bows, hands the card.

---

### 4️⃣ Business Card Etiquette (01:00‑01:20)
- **Tip 1:** Keep cards in a clean business card holder at your side.  
- **Tip 2:** Never jot notes on the front.  
- **Tip 3:** Store cards at the front of the desk, not a back pocket.

---

### 5️⃣ Customer Interaction (01:20‑02:00)

#### 5a. Language & Tone
- **Use “Hajimemashite”** (Nice to meet you) and “Arigatou gozaimasu” (Thank you).  
- **Avoid direct “no.”** Use “Shikaku o shite kudasai” (Let me see if we can change that).

#### 5b. Listening Skills
- **Nod & Pause** – let the customer finish.  
- **Offer Options, Not Orders** – “We can do… or we could also…”  

---

### 6️⃣ Inside the Car Wash (02:00‑03:00)

| Task | Quick Etiquette Tip | Why It Helps |
|------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1️⃣  **Greeting the customer** | “Good morning! Welcome to (Company). What can I help you with today?” | Sets a warm tone. |
| 2️⃣  **Showing the Car** | “Let me show you the features first.” | Engages the client. |
| 3️⃣  **Using the Wash Tools** | Keep tools clean; handle with both hands. | Demonstrates care. |
| 4️⃣  **Thank You** | “Thank you for choosing us, have a great day!” | Leaves a lasting positive note. |

---

### 7️⃣ Gift & Thank‑You Culture (03:00‑03:30)
- **Tipping is not common** – thank the customer with a small thank‑you note.  
- **Leave the car for the customer** – no pushing or cutting.  

---

### 8️⃣ Handling Difficult Situations (03:30‑04:00)
- Keep calm, use polite “onegai shimasu” (please), and apologize promptly.  
- If a customer is upset: **Listen first, offer a solution, never say “this is not my problem.”**

---

### 9️⃣ Quick Tips Recap (04:00‑04:30)
| # | Quick Cue | Result |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1 | Smile & say “Konnichiwa” | Trust |
| 2 | Wear a tidy uniform | Professionalism |
| 3 | Use a polite “sumimasen” when apologizing | Empathy |
| 4 | Never interrupt; let senior speak first | Harmony |
| 5 | Keep your mobile silent; no personal calls on‑shifts | Focus |

---

### 10️⃣ Closing (04:30‑05:00)
- **Presenter (camera close‑up):**  
  “Remember, a respectful attitude is the first wash we give. Let’s make every customer smile!”

---

### 11️⃣ Optional: Quick Quiz (Optional 02:00‑02:30)
1. What is the first thing you do when a customer enters?  
   • **A)** Show the menu.  
   • **B)** Bow.  
   • **C)** Offer a drink. → **Answer:** B

---

**End screen:**  
- Company logo  
- “Thank you for watching. For questions, contact your supervisor. Good luck!”  

--- 

**Ready to show?** Just cut between quick close‑ups of the etiquette checklist (bows, cards, gifts, card‑handling, card‑handing, etc.) and the script above to keep the video concise yet impactful.
